title: Fireplace TV Stand with Hidden Storage
summary: A plywood-and-1x12 TV console dressed with crown and base moulding to read as a built-in mantel — three door bays hide the storage behind a faux fireplace face.
description:
This is a plain bookshelf-style case underneath, but the applied moulding is what sells the "fireplace" illusion — crown at the top, a substantial base moulding at the bottom, and applied 1x2 trim around the door openings that does double duty as both styling and door handles. Building the top trim as a separate assembly first, then attaching the whole thing at once, is the one sequencing trick worth following: it's much easier to get the top perfectly aligned as a unit than to fuss with each piece individually while it's already attached to the case.

Euro-style non-mortise surface-mount hinges are a good choice here since they don't require cutting a hinge mortise into the door or case — install, adjust, done. Fit each of the three doors to its actual opening rather than the plan's stated size before committing to hinge placement.

tools.note: For mitering the crown and base moulding corners.

materials.name: Euro-style concealed hinges, non-mortise surface mount

cutList.note: Cut to fit the actual opening.
Cut to fit the actual opening.

steps.title: Cut the case parts
steps.body: Cut the ten 11-1/4"-wide case and door parts from the 1x12s: a 57-1/2" top, two 39-1/4" sides, a 52-1/2" bottom, two 33-3/4" dividers, a 31-1/2" center divider, two 31" side doors and a 31-1/4" middle door. There is exactly one layout that fits the boards you have — the 10 ft board gives 39-1/4" plus 39-1/4" plus 33-3/4", and the three 8 ft boards give 57-1/2" plus 33-3/4", then 52-1/2" plus 31-1/2", then 31" plus 31" plus 31-1/4" — so follow it rather than cutting the long pieces first and hoping. From the 1x3 cut a 57-1/2" top header and a 54" bottom header, and from the 1x6 a 54" footer. Leave the three doors slightly oversized: the cut list itself says to fit them to the actual openings, and you will not know those until the case is built.

steps.title: Build the bookshelf case
steps.body: Build the case as a plain bookshelf box: the 52-1/2" bottom fits between the two 39-1/4" sides and the 57-1/2" top caps over them. That is what the 54" x 40" back panel proves — 52-1/2" plus 3/4" of side at each end is 54", and 39-1/4" of side plus a 3/4" top is 40" — and it leaves the top overhanging 1-3/4" past each side, which the crown moulding later sits under. Pocket holes are convenient here but not required; wood screws from the assorted pack or 1-1/4" brad nails and glue hold just as well, since the applied moulding does most of the visual work. Stand the two 33-3/4" dividers and the 31-1/2" center divider inside to split the case into your door bays, spacing them to the door layout you have chosen and clamping a spacer between them while you fasten so they do not bow.

steps.title: Add the trim
steps.body: Build the top trim as its own assembly on the bench before it goes anywhere near the case — this is the one sequencing trick in the build that genuinely saves time. Join the 57-1/2" 1x3 top header, which matches the top's length, to the 54" 1x3 bottom header with brad nails and glue, keeping them parallel and their faces flush. Getting these aligned as a flat unit on a bench is far easier than fussing with each strip while it is already nailed to a case standing up.

steps.title: Attach the header assembly
steps.body: Add the 5-1/2" x 54" 1x6 footer to the assembly, then lift the whole trim unit onto the case and attach it in one operation. Register it to the case's top edge and to one end, and check that it sits level across the full 57-1/2" before you drive more than the first couple of fasteners. Attaching it as a unit is what keeps the top line straight; piece by piece, each strip picks up the last one's error.

steps.title: Fit and hang the doors
steps.body: Measure each of the three openings in the finished case and cut each door to its own opening — the openings will not be identical and the cut list's stated door sizes are a starting point, not a specification. For a surface-mount door, size each one to overlay its opening evenly on all four sides and leave a consistent gap between neighbors. Mount the Euro-style non-mortise hinges, which need no mortise cut in either the door or the case, then use their adjustment screws to bring the three doors into a straight line across the front; hang all three before you judge any one of them, since a fireplace face reads as a row.
materials: Euro-style concealed hinges, non-mortise surface mount

steps.title: Add the door trim
steps.body: Frame each door opening with the 1x2 trim, mitering or butting the corners consistently and fastening with 1" brad nails and glue. This trim is doing two jobs at once, so treat it as hardware and not just styling: it is what a hand grabs to open the door, so run it where a hand naturally reaches and leave enough of a lip proud of the door face to actually get fingers behind. Nail it into the door itself, not the case, or the doors will not open.

steps.title: Add crown and base moulding
steps.body: Miter the crown moulding corners and run it around the top, under the top's 1-3/4" overhang, fastening with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Cut and test-fit each outside corner on scrap first — crown is cut on the flat at a compound angle or nested against the saw fence, and a corner that is a degree off shows badly from across a room. Do the same with the 5-1/4" base moulding around the bottom. The substantial base and the crown together are what read as a built-in mantel rather than a bookshelf.

steps.title: Attach the back (optional)
steps.body: Cut the 1/4" back to 54" x 40", which is the case's actual outside width and height, and glue and nail it to the sides, the bottom, and every divider with 1" brad nails. Register a factory edge and corner of the panel to the case and pull the case into line with it as you nail — the back is what squares a 54" case, better than anything you can measure across the diagonal. The back is optional on this build, but without it the case will rack and the door gaps you just adjusted will not stay even.

images.alt: Fireplace-style TV stand with hidden storage behind three doors
